does: ConTrans introduces a novel framework for cost-efficient alignment transfer from smaller source models to larger target models through concept transplantation. The method operates via representation engineering by extracting concept vectors representing aligned values from a weak but aligned source LLM, refining these vectors, and reformulating them through affine transformation to match the target model's representation space. These transformed concept vectors are then injected into the residual stream of the target LLM. The authors demonstrate successful transfer of aligned concepts from 7B parameter models to 13B and 70B models across multiple LLM families and architectures, achieving performance that surpasses instruction-tuned baselines without expensive full alignment training.
connects: This work builds on representation engineering approaches to neural network interpretability and control, extending recent advances in mechanistic understanding of how LLMs encode concepts. It relates to mechanistic interpretability research that identifies and manipulates feature representations in transformer models, and connects to broader alignment literature addressing the computational expense of training large models to be safe and value-aligned. The weak-to-strong transfer framing draws conceptually from knowledge distillation and transfer learning paradigms, while the affine transformation approach echoes representation alignment techniques used in multi-model learning. The work implicitly responds to the growing recognition that full-model alignment training becomes prohibitively expensive at scale.
enables: ConTrans opens pathways for efficient alignment scaling where alignment properties learned on smaller, cheaper models can be transferred to frontier models without retraining from scratch. This could democratize alignment work by allowing smaller labs to align large models more affordably. Future work could explore whether transplanted concepts degrade over time, whether they generalize to out-of-distribution scenarios, and how multiple aligned concepts interact when simultaneously transplanted. The approach suggests deeper investigation into the geometry of value representations across model scales and architectures, potentially revealing universal structure in how aligned behavior is represented across LLMs, with implications for understanding and steering emerging capabilities.
